citations.narrative:
A review of police records from July 11, 2025, and results of this inspection, revealed that while being transported
eighteen dogs were subjected to conditions of excessive heat and poor sanitation that resulted in the death of four
animals. Police incident reports and a veterinarian statement describe four deceased dogs having matted hair coats from
urine and feces and suffering from dehydration due to exposure of excessive heat. Veterinary care was not provided to
the animals during the time of transportation. The veterinarian’s report concludes that heat related illness would have
been prevented if the dogs had access to a climate-controlled environment and humane housing. Necropsy findings
determined that death was due to heat stress.
Any person subject to the Animal Welfare regulations transporting dogs in commerce must ensure that the operator of the
conveyance, or a person accompanying the operator, observes the dogs as often as circumstances allow, but not less
than once every 4 hours, to make sure they have sufficient air for normal breathing, that the ambient temperature is within
the limits provided in § 3.16(e), and that all applicable standards of this subpart are being complied with. The regulated
person must ensure that the operator or person accompanying the operator determines whether any of the dogs are in
obvious physical distress and obtains any veterinary care needed for the dogs at the closest available veterinary facility.
The transporters cancelled their registrations at the time of inspection.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the registrants.
An OIG Special Agent was present at the time of inspection.
n

narrative:
3.18(a)  Critical
   
Care in transit. 
A review of police records from July 11, 2025, and results of this inspection, revealed that while being transported 
eighteen dogs were subjected to conditions of excessive heat and poor sanitation that resulted in the death of four 
animals. Police incident reports and a veterinarian statement describe four deceased dogs having matted hair coats from 
urine and feces and suffering from dehydration due to exposure of excessive heat. Veterinary care was not provided to 
the animals during the time of transportation. The veterinarian’s report concludes that heat related illness would have 
been prevented if the dogs had access to a climate-controlled environment and humane housing. Necropsy findings 
determined that death was due to heat stress. 
Any person subject to the Animal Welfare regulations transporting dogs in commerce must ensure that the operator of the 
conveyance, or a person accompanying the operator, observes the dogs as often as circumstances allow, but not less 
than once every 4 hours, to make sure they have sufficient air for normal breathing, that the ambient temperature is within 
the limits provided in § 3.16(e), and that all applicable standards of this subpart are being complied with. The regulated 
person must ensure that the operator or person accompanying the operator determines whether any of the dogs are in 
obvious physical distress and obtains any veterinary care needed for the dogs at the closest available veterinary facility.  
 
The transporters cancelled their registrations at the time of inspection. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the registrants. 
 
An OIG Special Agent was present at the time of inspection.
